Is Subsequence.name: Is Subsequence
Is Subsequence.notes:
This problem was my first encounter with the two-pointer technique. When I first tried it, I ran into a runtime error. I first thought that I did the problem wrong, but what I see now is just the simple mistake of not having edge cases.

This problem taught me the importance of edge case handling, proper boundary conditions, and code safety. The evolution from my first attempt to the final solution shows my growth in writing robust code. This code represents a successful solution and a valuable learning experience in my development journey. It shows my ability to learn from mistakes, iterate on solutions, and write 
code that handles edge cases gracefully. The progression from a bug-prone initial attempt to a robust final solution reflects my growth in writing production-quality code.

3Sum.name: 3Sum
3Sum.notes: This problem shows my progression with the two-pointer technique. After understanding the basics, this challenge introduced me to a more complex application. My first attempt showed an understanding of the core concept but missed crucial optimizations and duplicate handling. My mistakes were: didn't check if the first number was greater than 0 (which makes the sum impossible to be 0), didn't skip duplicate I values, and incorrectly moved both left and right pointers when finding three numbers that sum to 0, causing us to miss some combinations. My final solution demonstrates my more profound understanding of optimization techniques and further reinforces the importance of edge cases. Additionally, I learned that with more complex problems, the details of pointer movement and duplicate handling become crucial for correctness. This evolution in my understanding from a partially working solution to an optimized one shows my growth in writing more sophisticated algorithms.

The kth Factor of n.name: The kth Factor of n
The kth Factor of n.notes: This problem marks a significant shift in my approach to coding solutions. When I first solved it, I used an array list, following what I had learned from my introductory classes. While it worked, it wasn't very fast and needed to be more space used to extra space. After looking at the discussions, I realized that I didn't need to store all the answers and instead could have a counter variable. Here, I used math to find which position within the loop to return, as that is what we would return anyway. While this seems obvious now, it represents an important lesson in my development that sometimes the tools and patterns we learn in class aren't always the optimal choice for every problem. The evolution from using an ArrayList to just using a counter demonstrates my growth in writing more efficient code. This problem taught me to think beyond my classroom and consider time and space complexity when creating solutions. It's a small change that made a big difference in how I approach problems now
